original_abstract:
Interest in single-atom catalysts (SACs) continues to grow, yet a limited understanding of how these catalysts behave dynamically under operational conditions remains a major hurdle for their rational design. In this study, we investigate the evolution of active sites on a Pd/TiO₂-anatase SAC (Pd₁/TiO₂) during the reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) reaction. Through a combination of kinetic analysis, in situ characterization, and theoretical modeling, we demonstrate that exposure to H₂ at temperatures of 350 °C and above reduces the TiO₂ support and fundamentally alters the coordination environment of the Pd atoms. This process partially cleaves the Pd–O interfacial bonds and creates a unique electronic structure that exhibits high intrinsic rWGS activity via the carboxyl pathway.

This H₂-induced activation is accompanied by the partial sintering of isolated Pd atoms (Pd₁) into small, disordered, and flat clusters (~1 nm diameter, Pdₙ). While these highly active sites are eliminated by oxidation, we found that high-temperature oxidation can effectively re-disperse the Pdₙ clusters back into single atoms and facilitate further support reduction. In contrast, treatment with CO causes the Pd₁ species to sinter into large, crystalline nanoparticles (~5 nm, Pdₙₚ), leading to catalyst deactivation. During actual rWGS operation, these two evolution pathways compete. The H₂-driven activation mechanism ultimately dominates, resulting in an increasing reaction rate over time-on-stream and the formation of steady-state active sites similar to those generated under pure H₂. Our findings provide a detailed roadmap of how the coordination and nuclearity of SACs evolve during pretreatment and catalysis, offering critical insights into the structure-function relationships necessary for advancing catalyst design.
humanized_abstract:
Single-atom catalysts (SACs) have attracted enormous interest in recent years. However, a comprehensive understanding of their dynamic behaviors in working conditions remains a significant barrier for designing the active SACs. Here, we have investigated the changes of active sites on Pd/TiO2-anatase based single atom catalyst (Pd1/TiO2) during reverse water-gas shift (rWGS) reaction through combined kinetic study, in situ structural analysis, and density functional theory calculations. The results have revealed that, when Pd1/TiO2 is exposed to H2 at temperatures above 350 °C, the TiO2 anatase support will be reduced significantly, which results in the changed local environment around the Pd sites. After reduction, the Pd1 sites with partial broken Pd-O interface bonds exhibit an active electronic configuration that has high intrinsic rWGS activity in favor of the carboxyl route.

H2-induced activation and structural evolution of Pd single atoms with high activity and stability under rWGS Catalytic activation by H2 and structural reorganization of dispersed Pd single atoms with exceptional rWGS activity and stability The H2-induced activation and partial sintering of isolated Pd single atoms (Pd₁) into small, disordered, and flat clusters (~1 nm diameter, Pdₙ) are accompanied by their oxidation into a similar catalytically inactive Pdₙ state. High temperature oxidation can re-disperse the Pdₙ clusters into individual Pd single atoms and facilitate further support reduction. CO treatment of the catalyst leads to the sintering of Pd₁ into large, crystalline Pd nanoparticles (~5 nm, Pdⁿп). Competition between the two evolution pathways during rWGS catalysis leads to H2-induced activation mechanism dominating catalyst activity and increasing the rWGS reaction rate over time-on-stream. The desired catalytic active species are similar to those formed under pure H2. Understanding the detailed coordination and structural changes (nuclearity) of SACs during pretreatment and catalytic reactions and how they determine the overall catalytic performance is of great importance for the development of new catalytic materials.
